content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 30, 2000




Honorable Gail Benda
City Councilmember
City of Imperial Beach
825 Imperial Beach Boulevard
Imperial Beach, CA 91932

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-00-247

Dear Councilmember Benda:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as an
Imperial Beach City Councilmember under the 'conflict-of-interest provisions' of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").²

QUESTION

     May you participate in city council deliberations and decisions concerning the
city's evaluation of existing shore protection structures and possible implementation of a
new overall shoreline management strategy?

— CONCLUSION —

     You may not participate in city council deliberations and decisions concerning the
city's evaluation of existing shore protection structures and possible implementation of a
new overall shoreline management strategy due to your ownership of real property on the
oceanfront.


¹ Please note that the Commission is currently considering various amendments to the conflict-of-
interest regulations discussed below. You may wish to monitor the progress of these amendments on the
Commission's website (www.fppc.ca.gov). The advice contained herein is based on the law existing as of
the issue date of this letter.
² Government Code sections 81000 – 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-00-247
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a member of the Imperial Beach City Council. You also own real
property at 1324 Seacoast Drive, which is located on the oceanfront in the City of
Imperial Beach. In September 1999, the City submitted an application to the California
Coastal Commission for purposes of obtaining a grant to conduct a coastal engineering
study. The grant was subsequently awarded and the City solicited proposals for coastal
engineering services to conduct a study of the existing shore protection structures along
the oceanfront in order to develop an overall shoreline management strategy in Imperial
Beach. An agreement for the conduct of such a study was executed in July 2000.

The purpose of the study is to:

a) Develop an understanding of the various kinds of shore protection devices that
have been constructed in Imperial Beach;

b) Develop, if needed, an implementation plan that presents standards for new
devices, repair of existing devices, and calculation of sand mitigation fees when
determined appropriate;

c) Develop, if needed, a Shoreline Protection Ordinance that considers local beach
conditions and existing development patterns; and

d) Coordinate these procedures, if adopted, with the Local Coastal Program.

Public workshops are an integral part of the engineering study and the first of
such workshops was set for November 9, 2000.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the
prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides the
following eight-step analysis.

Step One: Are you a "public official?"

As a city councilmember you are a public official. (Section 82048; Reg.
18701(a).)

Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?

File No. A-00-247
Page No. 3

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Reg. 18702.1.) A public official "participates
in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and
without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or makes
recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Reg.
18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Reg. 18702.3.)

Your question concerns whether you may make and participate in decisions
concerning the shoreline management strategy. Thus, the focus of our analysis will be on
definitions set forth above that apply even where an official concludes that he or she has a
conflict of interest in a decision. Regulation 18702.4(a) (copy enclosed) provides in
pertinent part:

"(a) Making or participating in making a
governmental decision shall not include:

"...........................................

"(2) Appearances by a public official as a member
of the general public before an agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function to represent himself or
herself on matters related solely to the official's personal
interests as defined in Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18702.4(b)(1)...."

Regulation 18702.4(b) further provides in pertinent part:

"(b) Notwithstanding Title 2, California Code of
Regulations, section 18702.3(a), an official is not
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision of an agency covered by that
subsection if the official:

"(1) Appears in the same manner as any other
member of the general public before an agency in the
course of its prescribed governmental function solely to
represent himself or herself on a matter which is related to
his or her personal interests. An official's 'personal
interests' include, but are not limited to:

File No. A-00-247
Page No. 4

"(A) An interest in real property which is wholly
owned by the official or members of his or her immediate
family.

"(B) A business entity wholly owned by the official
or members of his or her immediate family.

"(C) A business entity over which the official
exercises sole direction and control, or over which the
official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole direction
and control.

"(2) Communicates with the general public or the
press."

Thus, for example, attending and participating in workshops to represent yourself
on matters related solely to your real property may be permissible if you meet the
requirements set forth above.

Step Three: Do you have an economic interest?

The third step is to identify the economic interests that may be impacted by the
decision. Under section 87100 of the Act, there are six different types of economic
interests that may result in a conflict of interest for a public official. The economic
interest about which you requested advice is your economic interest in real property. We
assume that your interest in the real property in question is worth $1,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b); Reg. 18703.2.)

Step Four: Is your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?

While determination of this step will depend on the substance of the specific
decision before the city council, at this time it appears that your property will be
indirectly involved in the decisions in question. Your real property is considered -
"directly involved" in a governmental decision under four circumstances:

"(1) The decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or
inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county, district or
other local governmental subdivision, of real property in
which the official has a direct or indirect interest (other
than a leasehold interest) of $1,000 or more, or a similar
decision affecting such property;

File No. A-00-247
Page No. 5

"(2) The decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement
authorizing a specific use or uses of such property;

"(3) The decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on
such property; or

"(4) The decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to
form a project area committee, to certify the environmental
document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory
to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions; and real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment area." (Reg. 18704.2(a).)

Since none of these definitions appear to apply, under regulation 18704.2(b) your
real property is considered to be indirectly involved in the decision.

Step Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on your economic interest
be material and reasonably foreseeable?

Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one of those economic interests. The official must find and apply the
applicable materiality standard set forth in Commission regulations (Reg. 18700(b)(5),
Reg. 18705, et seq.) and must decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
standard will be met. (Reg. 18700(b)(6).) Regulation 18706 provides that "A material
financial effect on an economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, within the meaning of
Government Code section 87103, if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards (see Cal. Code Regs., tit. 2, 18704, 18705) applicable to that
economic interest will be met as a result of the governmental decision." A financial
effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the other hand,
if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

In this case, your property is on the oceanfront which is the subject of the pending
decision. Where the official's real property is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
decision, the effect is presumed material unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official's real property interest. When the standard for materiality is "any
financial effect," the effect of decisions to preserve the shoreline will foreseeably meet
that standard. Thus, absent an exception, you have a conflict of interest, and except for

File No. A-00-247
Page No. 6

the actions set forth in the exceptions under step 2 above, you may not make, participate
in making, or influence the decisions concerning the shoreline project.3

Step Seven: Is the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your
economic interest distinguishable from the effect on the public generally?4

If the reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of a governmental decision
on your economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the "public generally,"
then you will not have a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Section 87103; Reg.
18707(a).) The reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on a public official's
financial interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally if it is also
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect a "significant segment" of the public
"in substantially the same manner" as it will affect the official's economic interest. (Reg.
18707(b)(1).)

For real property interests, a significant segment of the public may include:

"(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the
official represents, or

"(ii) Ten percent or more of all property owners, all
home owners, or all households in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency or the district the official represents, or

"...................................................................

"(C) ....5,000 individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction..." (Reg. 18707(b)(1)(A) and (C).)

You stated that approximately 297 individual property owners will be affected by
the decisions on the shoreline. This is far less than 10 percent of the city's population5
and less than the 5,000 individual raw number threshold. We do not have figures
concerning the number of property owners in the jurisdiction. However, we assume that
there are more than 2,970 property owners. If this is the case, the public generally
exception will not apply.

_________________
3 As the project progresses, decisions may focus on discrete portions of the shoreline. You may be
able to analyze each decision independently to determine if a conflict of interest exists. For example, if
one decision concerns a pier 400 feet from your real property, you would be subject to a less strict
materiality standard, and thus, might be able to participate in the decision. You should contact us for
further advice as the substance of the decisions become more clear.
4 We have not gone on to analyze the last step (step eight is whether you are legally required to
participate in the decision) since your facts do not suggest that you might be legally required to participate
in the decision. We have enclosed regulation 18708 for your information.
5 We understand from the city's website that the population is approximately 28,000.

File No. A-00-247
Page No. 7

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JWW:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\00247.doc

Enclosures: Reg. 18702.4 and 18708.

QUESTION: May you participate in city council deliberations and decisions concerning the
city's evalualion of existing shore protection structures and possible implementation of a
new overall shoreline management strategy?

CONCLUSION: You may not participate in city council deliberations and decisions concerning the
city's evaluation of existing shore protection structures and possible implementation of a
new overall shoreline management strategy due to your ownership of real property on the
oceanfront
I Please note that the Commission is currently considering various amendments to the conflict-of-
interest regulations discussed below. You may wish to monitor thl progress of these amendments on the
Commission's website (www.fppc.ca.gov). The advice contained herein is based on the law existing as of
the issue daG of this letter.
2Page No. 2
You are a member ofthe Imperial Beach city council. you also own rear
properfy at 1324 Seacoast Drive, *hi.h is located ; th; lceanfront in the city of
Imperial Beach' In septemb er 1,'999,the city submitteJan application to the bahfornia
coastal commission for purposes of obtaining a sr*ia ."lg1.t a coastal engineering
study' The grant was subsequently awarrlea ilatI" city soticitea proposals for coastal
engineering services to conduct u ttudy of the existing;ilore protection skuctures along
the oceanfront in order to develop an overall shorefinE management strategy in Imperial
Beach' An agreement for the conduct of such u rtuJy*u, executed in July 2000.
The purpose of the study is to:
a) Develop an understanding of the various kinds of shore protection devices that
have been constructed in Imperial Beach;
b) Develop, if needed, an implementation plan that presents standards for new
devices, repair of existing devices, and calculation of sand mitigation fees when
determined appropriate;
c) Develop' if needed, a Shoreline Protection ordinance that considers local beach
conditions and existing development patterns; and

Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 November 30, 2000 Honorable Gail Benda City Councilmember City of Imperial Beach 825 Imperial Beach Boulevard Imperial Beach, CA 91932 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-00-247 Dear Councilmember Benda: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding your duties as an Imperial Beach City Councilmember under the 'conflict-of-interest provisions' of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").² QUESTION May you participate in city council deliberations and decisions concerning the city's evaluation of existing shore protection structures and possible implementation of a new overall shoreline management strategy? — CONCLUSION — You may not participate in city council deliberations and decisions concerning the city's evaluation of existing shore protection structures and possible implementation of a new overall shoreline management strategy due to your ownership of real property on the oceanfront. ¹ Please note that the Commission is currently considering various amendments to the conflict-of- interest regulations discussed below. You may wish to monitor the progress of these amendments on the Commission's website (www.fppc.ca.gov). The advice contained herein is based on the law existing as of the issue date of this letter. ² Government Code sections 81000 – 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. A-00-247 Page No. 2 FACTS You are a member of the Imperial Beach City Council. You also own real property at 1324 Seacoast Drive, which is located on the oceanfront in the City of Imperial Beach. In September 1999, the City submitted an application to the California Coastal Commission for purposes of obtaining a grant to conduct a coastal engineering study. The grant was subsequently awarded and the City solicited proposals for coastal engineering services to conduct a study of the existing shore protection structures along the oceanfront in order to develop an overall shoreline management strategy in Imperial Beach. An agreement for the conduct of such a study was executed in July 2000. The purpose of the study is to: a) Develop an understanding of the various kinds of shore protection devices that have been constructed in Imperial Beach; b) Develop, if needed, an implementation plan that presents standards for new devices, repair of existing devices, and calculation of sand mitigation fees when determined appropriate; c) Develop, if needed, a Shoreline Protection Ordinance that considers local beach conditions and existing development patterns; and d) Coordinate these procedures, if adopted, with the Local Coastal Program. Public workshops are an integral part of the engineering study and the first of such workshops was set for November 9, 2000. ANALYSIS Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, regulation 18700 provides the
